Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd set aside time to study, maybe aim for a few hours each day, but I'd often end up just going through the motions without really absorbing much. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to watch videos and look at diagrams, but staying focused was a struggle. Some weeks I'd put in close to 20 hours, other weeks way less depending on how I was feeling.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot. I tried to have some consistency early on, but as the semester went on, I'd mostly study when deadlines were approaching or when I felt guilty about falling behind. There wasn't a solid weekly rhythm—more like bursts of activity followed by periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage much.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything was due soon, even if I didn't actually do any work while I was there. It felt more like an obligation than something I wanted to do most of the time.
Q4: For a brief period early in the course, I got into a habit of checking the LMS in the mornings with my coffee. But that routine fell apart pretty quickly when things started piling up and I felt overwhelmed. After that, logging in felt more random and reactive rather than part of any normal routine.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I could pause them, rewind, and really try to understand the diagrams and examples. The gamified tasks they introduced also helped a bit because they made things feel less monotonous and gave me small wins, which I needed given how unmotivated I was feeling.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion boards and reading materials. The readings felt dense and I'd lose focus quickly, so I'd skim them at best. The discussions seemed like extra effort that wouldn't directly help my grade, so I rarely participated beyond the bare minimum required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what would be on exams or assignments. If it was interactive or visual, even better. But if it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't impact my grade, I'd struggle to justify spending energy on it when I was already running low on motivation.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they were for students who had their lives together, not for me. Required ones I'd at least attempt because there were consequences, but optional stuff? I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did. There was this mental barrier where optional meant skippable.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and videos to my laptop. I'd study from those offline, especially when I didn't feel like being connected or when my focus was better away from all the distractions online. I also used some YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully grasp from the course materials.
Q10: There were definitely times I'd spend an hour or two going over downloaded slides and my handwritten notes, trying to make sense of things. None of that would show up in the LMS at all. I'd also sometimes study with a friend from the course in person, working through problems together without either of us being logged in.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch portions of it offline multiple times. Same with PDFs—I'd access them once to download, then read and annotate them on my tablet without ever going back to the LMS. My offline studying was probably more substantial than what the logs would suggest.
Q12: There was a stretch mid-semester where everything felt heavy. I wasn't stressed exactly, but I was sad and just disconnected from everything. Getting out of bed was hard enough, let alone engaging with coursework. My attendance dropped and I fell behind on assignments during that time.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor. I was juggling extracurriculars and taking eight online courses, which spread me thin. When I was tired, the LMS felt like this overwhelming thing I couldn't face. I'd open it, see all the tasks, and just close it again feeling defeated.
Q14: Many times. I'd log in, click through some materials, maybe start a video, but my mind would be elsewhere entirely. I was going through the motions without actually processing anything. It looked like engagement on paper but felt empty in reality.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed, part of it was this weird avoidance where starting felt harder than not starting. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd cram right before something was due, which meant my work wasn't my best. Without a deadline looming, I had no urgency, so I'd drift and procrastinate until panic mode kicked in.
Q17: When I got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, but often I'd read it and feel discouraged if it wasn't positive. Negative feedback made me want to avoid the course more rather than improve. I know that's not the healthy response, but that's honestly how it affected me.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually hurt me. Without rigid deadlines and mandatory check-ins, I'd let things slide. I needed more external structure to keep me accountable because my internal motivation just wasn't there this semester.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier—they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces and gave me a sense of progress. But the sheer volume of content and the length of some modules made it harder. I'd see a 45-minute video and immediately feel drained before even starting.
Q20: I think that could easily be someone like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching bits of videos, but never actually completing or submitting anything. It's like being present without being productive. You're there but not really there, you know?
Q21: That resonates somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show in the LMS. But honestly, my exam score shows that even with some offline effort, I wasn't performing well. So for me, low LMS activity matched low performance, unfortunately.
Q22: Course logs can show when and how often you accessed things, which gives a surface-level picture. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually focused, whether you understood anything, whether you were just clicking through mindlessly. They can't capture what's happening in your head or heart.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or logging in—it's being mentally present and motivated. This semester, I struggled with that. I was enrolled but not truly engaged most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad I felt throughout the semester or how that affected everything. It wouldn't show the times I stared at my screen unable to start, or the offline studying I did manage. It also wouldn't capture that the gamified tasks actually lifted my mood a bit, even if my overall performance didn't reflect that improvement.
